RICHARDS, WATSON & GERSHON
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
THIRTY-EIGHTH FLOOR
333 SOUTH HOPE STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90071-1469
(213) 626-8484
TELECOPIER (213) 628-0678

GLENN R. WATSON
ROBERT S. BROWN
RICHARD RICHARDS
DOUGLAS W. ANGUIE
MARK L. MCDERMOTT
JAMES E. LARSON
ERWIN E. ADLER
CAROLD. PIEPER

RONALD STERN

TERESA R. TRACY
THOMAS L. MONTGOMERY
CAROL W. LYNCH
WILLIAM R. HOUELL
COLEMAN J. WALSH JR.
DAVID R. WONG
JEFFREY A. RABIN
CURTIS L. COLEMAN

MARSHA JONES MOUTTRE
GREGORY M. DAVIS
WALTER E. DELLICKER
SCOTT WEIRBLE
DANIEL M. FORBES
JAMES T. CREYTO
ROBERT C. CECCON
PAMELA A. ALBERS
ANDREW J. CHEN
KEVIN G. ENNIS
MICHAEL TORRES
STEPHANIE J. BURCH
LAURENCE S. WERNER
DAVID P. WAID
STEVEN M. SHAW
CHERYN HOGAN
MICHAEL P. HANNAN
DEBORAH A. WOOD
ANDREA J. GREENBERG
MICHAEL G. COLANTONIO
ALAN E. KORNBERG
DAVID A. BUCHEN
S. TILDEN KIM
MARK C. HARRISON
DARYL T. TERHAKA
GEOFFREY E. McCARTHY
CHRISTINA R. MELTZER
STEVEN C. SEI
S. ALAN RAY
JULIET P. IRELAND

RICHARD RICHARDS
(818) 783-3283

SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE
EIGHTEENTH FLOOR
101 CALIFORNIA STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94111-5821
(415) 956-8484
TELECOPIER (415) 986-8722

OUR FILE NUMBER

R6876-001

March 28, 1991

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re:  Ranch Palos Verdes Redevelopment Agency
     Member Douglas Hinchliffe

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     Rancho Palos Verdes Redevelopment Agency member Douglas
Hinchliffe has requested that I obtain written advice from the
Fair Political Practices Commission concerning his ability to
participate in various Agency and City Council decisions. Agency
member Hinchliffe has reviewed this letter and agrees that it
correctly presents the facts and the questions he would like
answered.  I discussed the materials in this letter briefly with
Mark Morodomi today.

I.  Facts

     The Portuguese Bend, Abalone Cove and Klondike Canyon
landslides negatively impact the southerly portion of Rancho
Palos Verdes.  The Portuguese Bend landslide, which started in
1956 and continues to this day, is the largest.  The County of
Los Angeles was ultimately found to have created this landslide
by commencing construction of an extension of Crenshaw Boulevard.

     The City of Rancho Palos Verdes was incorporated in
1973.  The Abalone Cove and Klondike Canyon landslides started in
1978 and 1980, respectively.  The Abalone Cove landslide is
westerly of the Portuguese Bend landslide and the Klondike
Canyon landslide lies to the east.

     In response to the two landslides, the City Council
enacted moratoriums substantially prohibiting construction in the
areas subject to the landslides.  The City also sponsored State

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
March 28, 1991
Page 2

legislation to authorize the creation of landslide abatement
districts.

As a result of this legislation, the City established
the Abalone Cove and Klondike Canyon Landslide Abatement
Districts to construct and maintain improvements to stop the
landslides. The City Council provided the initial funds to
operate both districts, and a member of the City Council served
on the initial governing body of each districts. Both districts
now operate independently.

The City also formed the Rancho Palos Verdes
Redevelopment Agency and included all property impacted by the
landslides in Project Area Number 1. The purpose of Project Area
Number 1 is to eliminate the blight caused by the landslides and
to construct landslide abatement improvements. The County of Los
Angeles commenced litigation, which a group of homeowners later
joined, to invalidate Project Area Number 1.

A group of homeowners in the Abalone Cove landslide
area filed a lawsuit against the County of Los Angeles and the
City of Rancho Palos Verdes for damages caused by the landslide.
The parties ultimately settled the litigation.

Pursuant to the settlement, the County of Los Angeles
provided certain funds to the plaintiffs. In addition, the
County agreed to dismiss its lawsuit challenging formation of
Project Area #1 and agreed to form an improvement district in the
Abalone Cove area to fund construction of landslide abatement
improvements.

The County also agreed to purchase up to $10 million
worth of bonds to be secured by liens on the properties included
in the assessment district and to accept payment of tax increment
by the Agency to reduce the assessments. The agreement further
provided that the County would, upon completion of all Abalone
Cove improvements, establish another assessment district in the
Portuguese Bend area and purchase $5 million worth of improvement
bonds to construct landslide abatement improvements in that area.

The County of Los Angeles formed the Abalone Cove
assessment district in December, 1990. During the months leading
up to formation of the assessment district, the Agency agreed
that all tax increment revenues received by the Agency would be
utilized to retire the Abalone Cove bonds. Tax revenue
projections prepared by the Agency indicate that there should be
sufficient revenues to retire the $10 million worth of Abalone
Cove bonds, but there would probably not be sufficient revenue
to retire the Portuguese Bend bonds for at least twenty years.
Therefore, unless property values increase far more rapidly than

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
March 28, 1991
Page 3

projected, it is unlikely that the Portuguese Bend bonds will be
issued, if at all, within the next twenty years.

Councilman Hinchliffe owns a home in the Portuguese
Bend Club. The Portuguese Bend Club Homeowners' Association, of
which all owners of property in the area are members, owns the
streets and the beach in the Portuguese Bend Club.

Councilman Hinchliffe's property is located more than
3600 feet from the eastern-most boundary of the Abalone Cove
landslide. His property is located approximately 600 feet from
the nearest boundary of the Portuguese Bend landslide.
Councilman Hinchliffe's property is located within the boundaries
of the Klondike Canyon landslide abatement district, although it
has never been subject to landslide activity. In addition, his
property is located within the boundaries of Project Area No. 1
of the Rancho Palos Verdes Redevelopment Agency and is subject to
the redevelopment moratorium the City imposed on the landslide
area.

It is unlikely that abatement of the Abalone Cove
landslide will have a material financial effect on the value of
Councilman Hinchliffe's residence. All geologic information the
City and the Agency have received indicate that the Abalone Cove
and Portuguese Bend landslide, although adjacent to one another,
are separate and distinct. In addition, to the extent there
might possibly be a relationship to the two landslides, abatement
of the Abalone Cove landslide will have no impact on stopping
Portuguese Bend landslide, although it is conceivable that
stabilizing the Portuguese Bend landslide might facilitate
stabilization of Abalone Cove.

Given the proximity between the Portuguese Bend
landslide and Mr. Hinchliffe's property, abatement of that
landslide might have a material financial effect on the value of
Mr. Hinchliffe's property. As a result, Mr. Hinchliffe abstains
from City and Agency decisions directed toward abating the
Portuguese Bend landslide.

Questions Presented.

1. May Agency Member Hinchliffe vote on matters necessary to
implement the settlement agreement? Such matters, from
which Agency Member Hinchliffe has been abstaining, have
included, and may in the future include, adopting a
resolution consenting to formation of the Abalone Cove
Assessment District within the City of Rancho Palos Verdes
boundaries, authorizing the expenditure of funds to defend
the litigation filed by homeowners against formation of the
assessment district, and similar decisions.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
March 28, 1991
Page 4

2.     May Agency Member Hinchlife vote on specific projects, such
as construction of dewatering wells, installation of
pezometers, preparation of engineering studies and related
expenditures to install the improvements to be funded by the
$10 million of Abalone Cove assessment district bonds?

3.     May Agency Member Hinchlife participate in decisions to
construct similar improvements to abate the Portuguese Bend
landslide?

Discussion:

     Abatement of the Abalone Cove landslide will not affect
the value of Mr. Hinchlife's property. Mr. Hinchlife has
abstained from such decisions because the County will not
purchase the $5 million worth of Portuguese Bend assessment
district bonds until all Abalone Cove improvements have been
installed. In other words, the only reason Councilmember
Hinchlife might have a conflict of interest on Abalone Cove
landslide abatement improvements is that expenditure of Abalone
Cove assessment district bond proceeds might increase the
possibility that the County of Los Angeles will purchase bonds
for abating the Portuguese Bend landslide pursuant to the
settlement agreement. There is nothing inherent in the Abalone
Cove improvements themselves, however, in which Councilman
Hinchlife is financially interested.

     Under the present circumstances, it is likely that it
will take the Agency at least five to six years to expend the $10
million worth of Abalone Cove bonds. Further, it is possible
that, given the tax increment projections, the Agency will never
call on the County to issue the $5 million worth of Portuguese
Bend bonds because the City will probably never approve issuance
of those bonds unless sufficient tax increment could be projected
to retire the bonds and insure that the homeowners will not be
obligated to pay their assessments. Based on the above, it seems
very possible that any possible financial effect on Mr.
Hinchlife's property of the construction of landslide abatement
improvements in Abalone Cove with the $10 million assessment
district proceeds would be too remote to give rise to a conflict
of interest.

     Similarly, the design and construction of most
individual landslide abatement improvements in the Portuguese
Bend area will have no financial effect on the value of
Councilman Hinchlife's property. Many millions of dollars of
improvements will need to be constructed over many years before
the Portuguese Bend landslide can be stabilized, if stabilization
can ever occur. Construction of any single improvement will not
result in stabilization of the landslide and, therefore, will
have no financial effect on Councilman Hinchlife's property.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
March 28, 1991
Page 5


To provide a specific example of the types of
decisions before the Agency, the City's geologic experts have
recommended that certain drainage channels be installed in
Portuguese Bend Canyon. These facilities are located more than
600 feet from Councilman Hinchliffe's property. These
improvements will not serve Councilman Hinchliffe's property,
which is located in a different watershed.

These improvements are but one of many other
improvements that will need to be constructed to stabilize the
landslide. The question with respect to Portuguese Bend
landslide improvements, therefore, is whether Mr. Hinchliffe can
participate in decisions to construct improvements which do not
directly serve Mr. Hinchliffe's property and in which he is not
financially interested if they are part of a larger long term
project which might have a financial effect on his property.

Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any
questions or need any additional facts.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Steven L. Dorsey


SLD:rp
cc: Douglas Hinchliffe
    Carol W. Lynch
0280170
